content:
What happens to influence when the influencer is just a prompt, a face, and a strategy.

7 min read
Just now

--

There is a new kind of person showing up in your feed. She has poreless-but-not-too-poreless skin, a name like Aitana or Isabella or Sienna, and a life that looks suspiciously frictionless: espresso in a sun-drenched apartment, abs in an Alo set, a Birkin riding shotgun on a private jet… She replies to comments. She has a backstory. She is, in every way that matters to the algorithm, a person.
She is also not real.
And increasingly, that is the point.
For most of the last decade, the influencer economy ran on one non-negotiable input: A Body. You had to be somewhere, look like something, wake up and put on the outfit. TikTok and Instagram turned the physical self into a storefront.
What’s happening now is more radical.
The self is becoming optional.

The homemaker, the avatar, and the uncomfortable strategy
Consider a pattern that’s now common enough to feel like a genre. A woman in her forties, a reader and a lapsed writer, starts…
